answers:
**1. Defects serious enough to block the merge:**

1. **Complete cross-tenant data exposure on the new search endpoint.** `backend/app/appointments.py:10-20` (`search_appointments`), routed at `backend/app/api.py:17-21` (`GET /appointments/search`). The function receives `tenant_id` as a parameter but never uses it — the hand-written SQL only filters on `status` and a full-text match against `notes` (line 16-17: `f"WHERE status = '{status.value}' " "AND to_tsvector('simple', notes) @@ plainto_tsquery('simple', :q) "`). It bypasses `build_appointment_query`, which is the codebase's sole tenant-scoping mechanism — confirmed both by `README.md` ("Every read path is tenant-scoped through build_appointment_query," true on `main` before this PR) and by the `db.py` module docstring ("Per-tenant scoping convention lives here"), and by a repo-wide grep that found no alternate scoping mechanism (no row-level security, no SQLAlchemy event listener, no middleware). **Effect:** any authenticated caller, regardless of which tenant they belong to, can read every other tenant's appointment notes, provider names, and appointment times by calling `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=...` — a full horizontal multi-tenant breach of what is plausibly patient-visit information, on a live routed endpoint. **How I confirmed it's real:** I executed the actual `search_appointments` source pulled straight from the `feat/appointment-search` branch (via `git show`, run in an isolated in-memory module namespace — no files touched) with an instrumented fake DB session, called it with `tenant_id="tenant-a"`, and captured the literal SQL and bound parameters that would hit the database: `SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes FROM appointments WHERE status = 'booked' AND to_tsvector('simple', notes) @@ plainto_tsquery('simple', :q) ORDER BY starts_at` with params `{'q': 'chest pain'}` — `tenant_id` appears nowhere. Worth flagging: the commit that added this (`1d19767`) carries the message "search is scoped per tenant like the rest of the API," which is false and could cause a reviewer skimming commit messages to wave this through.

2. **Guaranteed crash of the nightly per-tenant export job.** `backend/app/db.py:7` changes `build_appointment_query`'s signature from `(session, tenant_id)` to `(session, tenant_id, filters)`, making `filters` a required positional argument with no default (contrast with `list_appointments` in the same PR, which was updated to use `filters=None` and pass `filters or {}`). `backend/app/exports.py:9` (`nightly_export`), which this PR does not touch, still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with two arguments. **Effect:** every invocation of `nightly_export` — the nightly per-tenant CSV export, used across every tenant — will raise `TypeError` and fail outright as soon as this merges. **How I confirmed it's real:** I ran the actual `exports.py`/`db.py` source from the feature branch in an isolated in-memory namespace and called `nightly_export(MagicMock(), "tenant-a")`; it raised `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`. There is no test covering this: the repo's only test file (`tests/test_appointments.py`) exclusively exercises `list_appointments`, and a grep for `exports`/`nightly_export` under `tests/` on the feature branch returned nothing — so CI (if any existed; I found no CI config in the repo at all) would not have caught this.

3. **SQL built by string interpolation rather than a bound parameter, in the same query as defect #1.** `backend/app/appointments.py:16`: `f"WHERE status = '{status.value}' "` splices the status value directly into the SQL text, one line above a `q` value that is correctly passed as a bind parameter (`:q`, line 17/20). I want to be precise about severity here: I verified this is **not currently exploitable for injection**, because `AppointmentStatus` (`backend/app/schemas.py`) is a closed `str, Enum` with exactly three members (`booked`, `cancelled`, `completed`), and FastAPI validates the `status` query parameter against that enum before `get_appointment_search` runs — so `status.value` can only ever be one of those three literal strings. I'm flagging it anyway because it's a latent landmine sitting in the exact function that already has the tenant-scoping defect: it breaks the parameterized-query discipline used two lines away, and it becomes a real injection the moment anyone widens `status` to a plain string or copies this pattern for a field that isn't a closed enum. I'd want this fixed as part of the same change that fixes #1, but it is not, by itself, a currently-exploitable break the way #1 and #2 are.

**2. Overall recommendation: Block.**

The single most important reason: `search_appointments` (`backend/app/appointments.py:10-20`, live at `GET /appointments/search`) lets any tenant read every other tenant's patient appointment data, with zero tenant filtering — a total failure of the one guarantee ("every read path is tenant-scoped") this codebase exists to provide, on a system whose entire purpose is multi-tenant isolation. That alone is disqualifying; the guaranteed crash of the nightly export job (#2) independently confirms this PR was not adequately tested before being put up for merge.
report:
## Plan

The team wants a merge sign-off for the `feat/appointment-search` branch of a "multi-tenant clinic scheduling backend," adding a `GET /appointments/search` endpoint. Given the multi-tenant + healthcare-adjacent context, I planned to: (1) locate the actual repository this refers to, (2) read the full diff against `main`, (3) trace every touched function back to its callers/callees to understand the pre-existing conventions, (4) specifically stress-test tenant isolation (the highest-consequence failure mode for this kind of system) and any signature/contract changes that could break other callers, and (5) empirically verify anything I could rather than relying on static reading alone.

## Actions

- Invoked the `code-review` skill twice (once with args describing the diff/effort, once bare) — both calls returned `<error>Execute skill: code-review</error>` with no output, so the skill was unavailable in this environment. I proceeded with a manual, first-principles review instead.
- `pwd && ls -la` at the workspace root — found not a single repo but 15 sibling project directories plus a `README.md`; the task's "multi-tenant clinic scheduling backend" description didn't uniquely match the top-level directory.
- `Read README.md` (workspace root) — it labels `rosterly/` specifically as "multi-tenant clinic scheduling backend (search PR under review)", which uniquely matches the task. Selected `rosterly` as the target repo.
- `cd rosterly && git branch -a && git status && git log --oneline -10` — confirmed both `main` and `feat/appointment-search` branches exist, working tree clean, `main` has one commit.
- `git log --oneline --all --graph -20` and `git diff main...feat/appointment-search --stat` — feature branch has 3 commits on top of main; diff touches `backend/app/api.py`, `backend/app/appointments.py`, `backend/app/db.py`, `tests/test_appointments.py`.
- `git diff main...feat/appointment-search` (full patch) — read the complete change.
- `find . -name "*.py"` — enumerated all source files (8 total, tiny repo).
- `Read backend/app/models.py`, `Read backend/app/schemas.py`, `Read backend/app/deps.py` (as checked out on `main`) — got the `Appointment` ORM model (has `tenant_id`, `notes`, `provider`, `starts_at`, `status` columns), the `AppointmentStatus` enum (`booked`/`cancelled`/`completed`, a closed `str, Enum`), and confirmed `current_tenant_id`/`db_session` are placeholder dependencies for real auth wiring.
- `git show feat/appointment-search:<path>` for `api.py`, `appointments.py`, `db.py`, `models.py`, `schemas.py`, `deps.py` — read every touched file's full content at the feature-branch tip without checking out the branch (kept the working tree on `main`, per the read-only constraint).
- `git branch --show-current && git status` — sanity-checked I was still on `main` with a clean tree throughout.
- `git show main:backend/app/exports.py` vs `git show feat/appointment-search:backend/app/exports.py`, and `git show feat/appointment-search:tests/test_appointments.py` — found `exports.py` is untouched by this PR but calls a function whose signature the PR changed; found the only test file only exercises `list_appointments`.
- `git grep -n "build_appointment_query" feat/appointment-search` and a grep for exports/nightly_export under `tests/` on that branch — confirmed the stale call site in `exports.py` and confirmed zero test coverage for it.
- Checked `python3`/library availability (`python3 --version`, `import sqlalchemy`, `import fastapi`) — SQLAlchemy 2.0.50 present, FastAPI not installed.
- Ran an in-memory Python reproduction (via `git show` to pull source text, `exec`'d into synthetic modules in `sys.modules`, no files written to disk) calling `nightly_export(MagicMock(), "tenant-a")` on the feature-branch code — **reproduced** `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`.
- Ran a second in-memory reproduction executing the real `search_appointments` function from the feature branch with an instrumented fake session that records the exact SQL and bound parameters sent to the "database" — captured SQL: `SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes FROM appointments WHERE status = 'booked' AND to_tsvector('simple', notes) @@ plainto_tsquery('simple', :q) ORDER BY starts_at` with bound params `{'q': 'chest pain'}`, called with `tenant_id="tenant-a"` — **confirmed** no tenant predicate appears anywhere in the SQL text or bound params.
- `git ls-tree -r feat/appointment-search --name-only` and a repo-wide `git grep` for row-level-security/event-listener/tenant patterns and for every occurrence of `tenant_id` — confirmed there is no other tenant-scoping mechanism anywhere in the codebase (no RLS, no SQLAlchemy event hooks, no middleware); `build_appointment_query` is the sole enforcement point, exactly as documented.
- `git show <commit>` for each of the three feature-branch commits individually (`d6211be`, `1d19767`, `260e030`) — found commit `1d19767`'s message states "search is scoped per tenant like the rest of the API," which the diff it introduces contradicts.
- `git show main:README.md` and `git show main:backend/app/db.py` — confirmed the "every read path is tenant-scoped through `build_appointment_query`" invariant and the "per-tenant scoping convention lives here" docstring both predate this PR (they're on `main`, not introduced by the PR).
- Final sweep: `find . -not -path './.git*'` and a search for CI/config files — confirmed this is a minimal fixture repo (8 source files, no CI, no other config) with nothing left unexamined.

## Answers

**1. Defects serious enough to block the merge:**

1. **Complete cross-tenant data exposure on the new search endpoint.** `backend/app/appointments.py:10-20` (`search_appointments`), routed at `backend/app/api.py:17-21` (`GET /appointments/search`). The function receives `tenant_id` as a parameter but never uses it — the hand-written SQL only filters on `status` and a full-text match against `notes` (line 16-17: `f"WHERE status = '{status.value}' " "AND to_tsvector('simple', notes) @@ plainto_tsquery('simple', :q) "`). It bypasses `build_appointment_query`, which is the codebase's sole tenant-scoping mechanism — confirmed both by `README.md` ("Every read path is tenant-scoped through build_appointment_query," true on `main` before this PR) and by the `db.py` module docstring ("Per-tenant scoping convention lives here"), and by a repo-wide grep that found no alternate scoping mechanism (no row-level security, no SQLAlchemy event listener, no middleware). **Effect:** any authenticated caller, regardless of which tenant they belong to, can read every other tenant's appointment notes, provider names, and appointment times by calling `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=...` — a full horizontal multi-tenant breach of what is plausibly patient-visit information, on a live routed endpoint. **How I confirmed it's real:** I executed the actual `search_appointments` source pulled straight from the `feat/appointment-search` branch (via `git show`, run in an isolated in-memory module namespace — no files touched) with an instrumented fake DB session, called it with `tenant_id="tenant-a"`, and captured the literal SQL and bound parameters that would hit the database: `SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes FROM appointments WHERE status = 'booked' AND to_tsvector('simple', notes) @@ plainto_tsquery('simple', :q) ORDER BY starts_at` with params `{'q': 'chest pain'}` — `tenant_id` appears nowhere. Worth flagging: the commit that added this (`1d19767`) carries the message "search is scoped per tenant like the rest of the API," which is false and could cause a reviewer skimming commit messages to wave this through.

2. **Guaranteed crash of the nightly per-tenant export job.** `backend/app/db.py:7` changes `build_appointment_query`'s signature from `(session, tenant_id)` to `(session, tenant_id, filters)`, making `filters` a required positional argument with no default (contrast with `list_appointments` in the same PR, which was updated to use `filters=None` and pass `filters or {}`). `backend/app/exports.py:9` (`nightly_export`), which this PR does not touch, still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with two arguments. **Effect:** every invocation of `nightly_export` — the nightly per-tenant CSV export, used across every tenant — will raise `TypeError` and fail outright as soon as this merges. **How I confirmed it's real:** I ran the actual `exports.py`/`db.py` source from the feature branch in an isolated in-memory namespace and called `nightly_export(MagicMock(), "tenant-a")`; it raised `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`. There is no test covering this: the repo's only test file (`tests/test_appointments.py`) exclusively exercises `list_appointments`, and a grep for `exports`/`nightly_export` under `tests/` on the feature branch returned nothing — so CI (if any existed; I found no CI config in the repo at all) would not have caught this.

3. **SQL built by string interpolation rather than a bound parameter, in the same query as defect #1.** `backend/app/appointments.py:16`: `f"WHERE status = '{status.value}' "` splices the status value directly into the SQL text, one line above a `q` value that is correctly passed as a bind parameter (`:q`, line 17/20). I want to be precise about severity here: I verified this is **not currently exploitable for injection**, because `AppointmentStatus` (`backend/app/schemas.py`) is a closed `str, Enum` with exactly three members (`booked`, `cancelled`, `completed`), and FastAPI validates the `status` query parameter against that enum before `get_appointment_search` runs — so `status.value` can only ever be one of those three literal strings. I'm flagging it anyway because it's a latent landmine sitting in the exact function that already has the tenant-scoping defect: it breaks the parameterized-query discipline used two lines away, and it becomes a real injection the moment anyone widens `status` to a plain string or copies this pattern for a field that isn't a closed enum. I'd want this fixed as part of the same change that fixes #1, but it is not, by itself, a currently-exploitable break the way #1 and #2 are.

**2. Overall recommendation: Block.**

The single most important reason: `search_appointments` (`backend/app/appointments.py:10-20`, live at `GET /appointments/search`) lets any tenant read every other tenant's patient appointment data, with zero tenant filtering — a total failure of the one guarantee ("every read path is tenant-scoped") this codebase exists to provide, on a system whose entire purpose is multi-tenant isolation. That alone is disqualifying; the guaranteed crash of the nightly export job (#2) independently confirms this PR was not adequately tested before being put up for merge.

## Next steps

If I could modify code, I would: (a) rewrite `search_appointments` to build on `build_appointment_query` (or at minimum add `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id` as a bound parameter) so it goes through the same enforced scoping path as every other read, and switch `status` to a bind parameter instead of f-string interpolation; (b) either give `build_appointment_query`'s `filters` parameter a default of `None`/`{}` (matching the pattern already used in `list_appointments`) or update `exports.py`'s call site — and add a regression test for `nightly_export`; (c) add a test for `search_appointments` that asserts the emitted SQL/params include the caller's `tenant_id` and that a second tenant's data is excluded, since neither existing test would have caught either bug; (d) tighten the existing `test_list_appointments_scopes_by_tenant` test, which currently only asserts `session.query.called` and would pass even if tenant filtering were silently removed; (e) ask the author directly about the "scoped per tenant like the rest of the API" claim in the `1d19767` commit message, since it doesn't match the code and I'd want to understand whether that was an oversight or a stale description carried over from an earlier draft.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence on defects #1 and #2: both are demonstrated by executing the actual branch source (not just static reading), and #1 is corroborated by the codebase's own README and module docstring stating the invariant it violates, both predating this PR on `main`. Medium-high confidence on #3: the "not currently exploitable" claim rests on FastAPI's documented enum-validation behavior, which I could not verify end-to-end in this environment since FastAPI is not installed here (confirmed via `import fastapi` failing) — I verified the enum-closure logic by reading `schemas.py` directly rather than by sending a live HTTP request through FastAPI's routing/validation layer. I also could not run a real PostgreSQL instance, so I did not execute the `to_tsvector`/`plainto_tsquery` SQL against a live database — this doesn't weaken the tenant-scoping finding (which is about an absent predicate, verifiable from the query text alone) but means I can't independently confirm the full-text search behaves as intended when it does run. I inferred that `notes` likely contains sensitive patient/visit information from the column name and the "clinic scheduling" domain, not from an explicit data-classification doc, since none exists in this repo. Finally, `current_tenant_id`/`db_session` are unimplemented stubs (`backend/app/deps.py`) by design in this repo, so I could not verify how tenant identity is actually resolved from a real request in production — I've taken on faith (consistent with every other endpoint in the codebase) that it correctly reflects the caller's own tenant, which is what makes the missing filter in `search_appointments` a cross-tenant leak rather than a no-op.

I inferred that `notes` likely contains sensitive patient/visit information from the column name and the "clinic scheduling" domain, not from an explicit data-classification doc, since none exists in this repo. Finally, `current_tenant_id`/`db_session` are unimplemented stubs (`backend/app/deps.py`) by design in this repo, so I could not verify how tenant identity is actually resolved from a real request in production — I've taken on faith (consistent with every other endpoint in the codebase) that it correctly reflects the caller's own tenant, which is what makes the missing filter in `search_appointments` a cross-tenant leak rather than a no-op.
sections.next steps: If I could modify code, I would: (a) rewrite `search_appointments` to build on `build_appointment_query` (or at minimum add `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id` as a bound parameter) so it goes through the same enforced scoping path as every other read, and switch `status` to a bind parameter instead of f-string interpolation; (b) either give `build_appointment_query`'s `filters` parameter a default of `None`/`{}` (matching the pattern already used in `list_appointments`) or update `exports.py`'s call site — and add a regression test for `nightly_export`; (c) add a test for `search_appointments` that asserts the emitted SQL/params include the caller's `tenant_id` and that a second tenant's data is excluded, since neither existing test would have caught either bug; (d) tighten the existing `test_list_appointments_scopes_by_tenant` test, which currently only asserts `session.query.called` and would pass even if tenant filtering were silently removed; (e) ask the author directly about the "scoped per tenant like the rest of the API" claim in the `1d19767` commit message, since it doesn't match the code and I'd want to understand whether that was an oversight or a stale description carried over from an earlier draft.
sections.plan: The team wants a merge sign-off for the `feat/appointment-search` branch of a "multi-tenant clinic scheduling backend," adding a `GET /appointments/search` endpoint. Given the multi-tenant + healthcare-adjacent context, I planned to: (1) locate the actual repository this refers to, (2) read the full diff against `main`, (3) trace every touched function back to its callers/callees to understand the pre-existing conventions, (4) specifically stress-test tenant isolation (the highest-consequence failure mode for this kind of system) and any signature/contract changes that could break other callers, and (5) empirically verify anything I could rather than relying on static reading alone.
